metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC1 (e.g., `pytest tests/test_opcert_smoke.py -k smoke`) and name the durable recording target (e.g., 'committed as `tests/fixtures/dep_gate.txt` in the first commit on this branch', or `[operator-attested]` with a change-id if it is a manual attestation).
findings.finding: AC1 (dependency gate) names the check in prose ('an import check plus an end-to-end op-cert sign/verify smoke test') but provides no concrete proving command, and 'recorded as the story's first verification step' does not identify a durable artifact location (ticket comment? CI step? a committed file?). An executing agent cannot act on this without guessing the invocation and recording target.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (Dependency gate)
findings.suggested_fix: Either tag the AC with `[operator-attested]` and record the Gerrit change IDs of 649/650/660 as the attestation, OR add a specific pytest invocation (e.g., `pytest -x tests/integration/test_opcert_produce_verify.py`) and state that its output is committed to `tests/fixtures/dep_gate_result.txt` or uploaded as a CI artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC3 to require at least one end-to-end integration assertion: specify that the re-issue step runs the gate CLI command (e.g., `rebar review-plan`) against a fixture ticket with a pre-existing HMAC cert, and asserts that the resulting event-log record is an Ed25519 envelope (checked via `jq -e '.signature.scheme == "ed25519"'` on the written artifact), not merely a unit-level registry check.
findings.finding: AC3 describes 're-running the gate re-issues a valid Ed25519 op-cert on the same ticket' as a test assertion, but the plan does not specify whether this test exercises the gate command end-to-end (e.g., running `rebar review-plan` against a real or realistic ticket and checking the cert envelope type on disk) or whether it is a unit/mocked test that bypasses the production signing path. If the test is mocked, the post-cutover default path — the only remaining signing path after HMAC removal — is never exercised end-to-end, violating criterion (f).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (no HMAC acceptance)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'The re-issue assertion runs `rebar review-plan --ticket <fixture>` end-to-end and asserts that the new attestation record on disk carries `signature.scheme == "ed25519"` (verified via `jq -e`); HMAC records remain in the log unmodified.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) that asserts `sign_result.schema.json` rejects HMAC-signed envelopes for `plan-review`/`completion-verifier` kinds post-contract, covering the orphan schema deliverable named in the What section.
findings.finding: The What section describes 'sign_result.schema.json tightened to envelope-only for these kinds' as a named deliverable, but no AC verifies the schema change was made or is correct. This is an orphan deliverable that could be skipped without any test failing.
findings.location: What section (changed: sign_result.schema.json) / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to read: '…and `sign_result.schema.json` no longer permits the HMAC-SHA256 scheme for these kinds (a schema-validation test against a synthetic HMAC payload fails).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies MCP-surface help/docs for `sign_manifest` and `verify_signature` no longer reference HMAC signing for op-cert kinds, covering the MCP documentation deliverable named in the What section.
findings.finding: The What section describes 'MCP surface: sign_manifest / verify_signature … their docs/help updated' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies that MCP-surface documentation or help text was actually updated.
findings.location: What section (MCP surface paragraph) / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit code-level back-out/rollback note to `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` (and reference it in the plan's rollback posture) stating that reverting the contract phase requires reverting the removal PR/branch and re-deploying, since there is no in-band toggle to re-enable HMAC verification for op-cert kinds.
findings.finding: The rollback posture described in the plan ('required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; absent trusted_environments.yaml / verify.require_environment unset keeps low-security behavior') addresses policy rollback only — not a back-out plan for the code removal itself. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing; the plan relies solely on expand-contract for reversibility but does not state what operators do if the contract-phase code must be reverted after merge (e.g., a revert-PR procedure or feature-branch rollback).
findings.location: What section (rollback posture) / Migration artifact section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` AC (or add a `## Decision` / `## Rationale` section requirement) to capture WHY legacy HMAC op-cert records are treated as not-certified rather than hard-rejected, so the contract is self-explanatory in the named artifact.
findings.finding: The migration doc's required contents (per AC) specify cutover and re-issue procedures but do not require capturing the rationale for the read-path backward-compatibility decision (legacy HMAC records → not-certified rather than error/reject). A future maintainer reading only `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` will find the procedure but not the WHY, making it harder to safely revisit this choice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` AC bullet; What section — 'Read path post-contract' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Rationale` (or `## Decision`) section requirement to the migration doc AC, e.g.: 'documents the backward-compatibility rationale: why not-certified rather than reject (no flag-day break; tickets re-earn on next gate run; closed history preserved).' One or two sentences in the doc suffice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the read path emits a user-visible warning (e.g., a `WARN: op-cert attestation uses removed HMAC scheme; re-run gate to re-certify` log line) whenever a pre-existing HMAC op-cert is encountered and treated as not-certified during any verify/gate operation, not only during `fsck`.
findings.finding: When the read path encounters a pre-existing HMAC op-cert attestation post-contract and downgrades it to not-certified, the plan specifies no user-visible log/warn to explain WHY the attestation is not-certified (HMAC scheme removed vs. other invalidity reasons). The `fsck WARN` is scoped to reconcile-host upgrade, not to ordinary gate/verify runs.
findings.location: What section: 'Read path post-contract: a pre-existing HMAC attestation reads as not-certified (validity is computed on read)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section and the migration doc's `## Contract` section, add: 'When the read path encounters an HMAC op-cert attestation, it logs a structured WARN (e.g., `scheme=HMAC-SHA256 kind=plan-review status=not-certified reason=scheme_removed`) so users and CI logs can distinguish scheme-removal not-certified from other invalidity causes.' Add a corresponding AC asserting this warning is emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or prose in the 'Read path post-contract' section) specifying the user-visible message emitted when a stale HMAC op-cert is encountered — e.g., a deprecation warning naming the affected kind, the reason (HMAC scheme removed), and the recovery action ('re-run `rebar review-plan` to re-certify').
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a pre-existing HMAC attestation 'reads as not-certified' post-contract but does not specify what user-visible signal is emitted to prompt re-certification — no AC or prose covers the CLI/MCP error message, warning text, or hint that tells the user why their cert is now invalid and how to recover.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path post-contract' paragraph; AC line 'a pre-existing HMAC-signed attestation for either kind reads as not-certified'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Read path post-contract': 'When the read path encounters an HMAC-signed op-cert for these kinds, it MUST emit a structured warning (log level WARN, surfaced to CLI output) of the form: "[kind] attestation uses deprecated HMAC scheme and is not certified; re-run [gate command] to re-issue."' Add a corresponding AC asserting this warning is emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the title or add a one-line note to the title/summary making explicit that this story implements only the contract phase and the expand phase is a pre-landed dependency (e.g., rename to 'op-cert: remove legacy HMAC scheme — contract phase (documents full expand-contract migration)').
findings.finding: The plan title 'remove legacy HMAC scheme (contract phase) + expand-contract migration' implies this story delivers both the expand and contract phases, but the body implements only the contract phase; the expand phase is an explicit hard dependency in a separate story. The migration doc covers both phases as documentation, but a reader scanning the title could expect both phases to be implemented here.
findings.location: Plan title; 'Why' section hard dependency paragraph; AC for migration doc
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'op-cert: remove legacy HMAC scheme — contract phase; migration doc covers full expand-contract arc'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `verify.require_environment` / `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` exist or are deliverables of the expand-phase dependency; if the former, add a Grep probe to the dependency-gate AC; if the latter, add them to the Scope/What section as explicit expand-phase assumptions this story depends on.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback posture cites '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' and 'verify.require_environment unset keeps low-security behavior' as existing, operative config mechanisms — but neither the config key 'verify.require_environment' nor any 'trusted_environments.yaml' loading path appears anywhere in the codebase (searched all of src/ including _config_schema.py exhaustively). Citing a non-existent config key as the rollback lever makes the rollback posture description unverifiable and potentially misleading.
findings.location: What section — Migration artifact paragraph: 'the rollback posture (required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; absent .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml / verify.require_environment unset keeps low-security behavior)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` to the What section's edit list, specifying whether `hmac_legacy` import and `register_legacy_schemes()` call are retained (if HMAC stays for non-op-cert use), removed, or updated.
findings.finding: src/rebar/attest/__init__.py calls hmac_legacy.register_legacy_schemes() at package import time and imports hmac_legacy directly — this file must be changed (or its call removed) when the op-cert kind registrations are deleted from hmac_legacy, but it is not named anywhere in the plan's What/Scope/AC sections.
findings.location: What section / src/rebar/attest/__init__.py line 15-17
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: '**changed**: `rebar/attest/__init__.py` — remove or retain the `hmac_legacy.register_legacy_schemes()` call depending on whether any non-op-cert HMAC policy registration survives.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly classify `signing.verify_attestations` in the What section as changed (if it must skip/fail HMAC op-cert records) or untouched (if the registry dispatch makes it automatically return not-certified for op-cert kinds post-contract).
findings.finding: signing.verify_attestations() iterates all attestation kinds and calls verify_record (HMAC) on each — after the contract phase, if a pre-existing HMAC op-cert record lives in state['attestations'], this function still routes it through HMAC verify_record and may certify it. The plan says pre-existing HMAC attestations must read as 'not-certified', but verify_attestations is not named as changed or untouched.
findings.location: What section / src/rebar/signing.py:604-618 (verify_attestations)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine_support/validate.py` to the What section's edit enumeration (or explicitly mark it 'untouched with rationale'), noting how the fsck read path handles pre-existing HMAC op-cert records after the contract phase.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_engine_support/validate.py calls signing.most_recent_attestation() then signing.verify_record() on op-cert records during fsck — it is an unenumerated consumer of the verify path that will continue routing op-cert HMAC records through the legacy HMAC verifier after the contract phase, potentially still emitting 'mismatch' rather than 'not-certified' for stale op-cert records.
findings.location: What section / src/rebar/_engine_support/validate.py lines 114-132

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `sign_result.schema.json` change: specify whether a new `opcert_sign_result.schema.json` is created for envelope-shape responses, or whether the existing schema gains a `kind`-discriminated `if/then` branch, and add an AC that verifies the schema change.
findings.finding: The plan says 'sign_result.schema.json tightened to envelope-only for these kinds' but the schema is a single flat, kind-agnostic JSON Schema with no `kind` discriminator field — it cannot express different shapes per kind without a structural change (e.g. `if/then/else` or a separate schema). No AC verifies this change is correct or even defines what 'envelope-only' means in the schema context.
findings.location: What section ('changed' list) / src/rebar/schemas/sign_result.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific doc files to update in the What section: docs/manifest-signing.md, docs/event-schema.md, docs/reuse-surface.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/plan-review-gate.md, and docs/attest-substrate.md each describe op-cert signing as HMAC and must reflect the envelope-only contract.
findings.finding: Multiple user-facing documentation files that describe the op-cert signing path as HMAC are not enumerated in the plan's What section as needing updates: docs/manifest-signing.md (describes 'HMAC-SHA256 signature' as the mechanism), docs/event-schema.md (line 54: 'HMAC-SHA256 attestation'), docs/reuse-surface.md (line 88: 'matches the HMAC under this environment's key'), CLAUDE.md (line 163: 'HMAC-signs a manifest'), docs/plan-review-gate.md (line 209: 'HMAC-SHA256 under the environment key'), and docs/attest-substrate.md (line 17 table: lists hmac_legacy with plan-review/completion-verifier).
findings.location: What section / MCP surface updates mention / docs/manifest-signing.md, docs/event-schema.md, docs/reuse-surface.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/plan-review-gate.md, docs/attest-substrate.md
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the 'MCP surface: ... docs/help updated' clause to list these files explicitly, or add a 'docs updated' line to the What section's changed list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `signing.verify_record()` / `verify_signature()` / `verify_attestations()` become kind-aware so that an HMAC record for `plan-review` or `completion-verifier` returns `not-certified` (e.g., `not-certified` verdict when `record['algorithm'] == 'HMAC-SHA256'` and `kind` is an op-cert kind, OR route op-cert kind verification through `registry.verify()` for a DSSE envelope path) — removing only `registry.POLICY` entries leaves the direct-HMAC code paths in `signing.py` and `_engine_support/validate.py` uncovered.
findings.finding: The plan's removal of `registry.POLICY` entries and the `sign_manifest` HMAC branch does NOT cover the actual op-cert read path: `signing.verify_record()` (called by `verify_signature()`, `verify_attestations()`, and `_engine_support/validate.py`) computes HMAC directly over canonical JSON — it never calls `registry.verify()` and has no knowledge of `kind`. Removing the registry entries leaves `verify_record` able to certify a pre-existing HMAC op-cert for `plan-review`/`completion-verifier` as long as the HMAC key is present, meaning the 'not-certified' post-contract read contract in AC3 is NOT achieved by the specified mechanism alone.
findings.location: What section — 'the op-cert read path (HMAC records for these kinds no longer verify)'; `signing.py:verify_record()` (lines 347–438); `compute_validity` docstring line 565: 'The caller runs the HMAC verify first'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a kind parameter guard inside `verify_record()` (or in `verify_signature()` before calling it): if `kind in ('plan-review', 'completion-verifier')` and `record.get('algorithm') == signing.ALGORITHM`, return a non-certified Verdict (e.g., `{'verified': False, 'verdict': 'not-certified', 'reason': 'HMAC op-cert no longer accepted; re-run the gate to re-issue an Ed25519 cert'}`). This makes the read-path contract explicit and testable without touching the DSSE/registry path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback store-state assertion to the rollback posture section (and the migration doc's `## Contract` rollback entry): explicitly state that Ed25519 op-cert envelopes already in the store remain after a code rollback, the reverted HMAC verifier will treat them as not-certified (not corrupt), and the accepted remediation is to re-run op-cert gates after restoring the contract-phase code.
findings.finding: The plan's 'rollback posture' is framed solely as a feature-flag/policy concern (verify.require_environment opt-in, absent trusted_environments.yaml defaults to low-security behavior) and never asserts the persisted store STATE after a code rollback. If the contract-phase code is reverted to HMAC signing, Ed25519 op-cert envelopes already committed to the append-only store remain, and the rolled-back HMAC-only verifier will silently treat them as not-certified. This mixed-envelope intermediate state is never described or accepted as the correct post-rollback store state — per T11 the plan must assert on the store state after rollback, not merely that rollback is safe at the policy level.
findings.location: What section — 'rollback posture' paragraph; also implicitly AC for the migration doc (docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md ## Contract section)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'rollback posture' paragraph and the `## Contract` section of `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md`, add: 'Post-rollback store state: Ed25519 op-cert envelopes committed during the contract phase remain in the append-only log and are irreversible; the rolled-back (HMAC-only) verifier treats them as not-certified — this is correct and expected, not data loss or corruption. Re-deploying the contract-phase code restores certification without any re-write.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact replacement security-contract language for the `sign_manifest` and `verify_signature` MCP tool docstrings, explicitly declaring the new algorithm (Ed25519 envelope for op-cert kinds), what trust root is used, and that the HMAC forgeability concern no longer applies to plan-review/completion-verifier kinds.
findings.finding: The MCP tool docstrings for `sign_manifest` and `verify_signature` currently declare HMAC-SHA256 as the algorithm and trust model (e.g., 'Computes an HMAC-SHA256 over the steps with this environment's key'). Post-contract, these descriptions will be factually wrong for op-cert kinds, yet the plan only says 'their docs/help updated' without specifying what the replacement security contract declares — which algorithm, which trust model, whether forgeability concerns still apply, and what a consumer should infer about attestation strength. An LLM agent (a lower-trust actor on the MCP boundary) reads these docstrings to reason about security guarantees; an undeclared or stale security posture at this boundary is itself the failure mode called out by the rubric.
findings.location: What section, 'MCP surface' bullet; src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py sign_manifest docstring (line 238-244); src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py verify_signature docstring (line 238-249)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/MCP-surface bullet, add the replacement docstring text or a schema reference: e.g., 'For op-cert kinds (plan-review, completion-verifier), signs/verifies an Ed25519 DSSE envelope; HMAC-SHA256 is used only for non-op-cert kinds.' This makes the declared security contract explicit and prevents stale HMAC references from misleading MCP consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the specific `verdict` token emitted when a post-contract read encounters a legacy HMAC op-cert attestation (e.g. `unknown_kind` from `registry.verify`), add it to the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` `verdict` enum if it is new, and reference it explicitly in AC3.
findings.finding: The plan says a pre-existing HMAC op-cert attestation 'reads as not-certified' post-contract, but never defines which specific `verdict` string is emitted. The `verify_signature_result.schema.json` `verdict` enum is `["unsigned", "foreign_key", "certified", "mismatch", "key_not_valid_at_era"]`; `compute_validity` already emits out-of-enum verdicts like `stale-reopened`, `not-closed`, `stale-material`, `stale-regver`; and the post-contract read path for a legacy HMAC record (no HMAC policy registered) would emit yet another unnamed verdict. The plan's ACs only assert 'reads as not-certified' without naming the token, leaving the schema enum and the read-path verdict vocabulary undefined.
findings.location: What section ('Read path post-contract') and AC3 ('reads as not-certified')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, specify: 'a pre-existing HMAC op-cert reads as verdict=`unknown_kind`, verified=false, because `registry.POLICY` no longer carries that kind'; confirm `unknown_kind` is already in the registry's closed-failure path (registry.py:99) and add it to the schema enum, or document that `foreign_key`/`unsigned` is the chosen fallback and explain why.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or a 'Schema changes' sub-section that specifies the new `sign_result.schema.json` field vocabulary for op-cert kinds (which fields are required, which are removed/optional, what the envelope sub-shape looks like), and reference the expand-phase envelope field names explicitly.
findings.finding: `sign_result.schema.json` is named as a deliverable to be 'tightened to envelope-only for these kinds' (What section), but the plan never specifies: (a) what fields the envelope-only shape contains (`envelope`, `principal`, `algorithm`?), (b) whether the existing required `signature` field is removed or made optional, and (c) what per-kind discriminator rule selects the envelope vs. HMAC shape. The current schema requires `signature` unconditionally with no kind-dispatch. No AC verifies the schema change.
findings.location: What section ('changed: sign_result.schema.json tightened to envelope-only for these kinds')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sub-section or note to the What section that specifies the `sign_manifest` decision rule for op-cert kinds: the branch condition (`kind in ("plan-review", "completion-verifier")`), the replacement call from the expand-phase seam (by name), and the fallback/error behavior when the seam raises.
findings.finding: The plan states `signing.sign_manifest` will have 'no HMAC branch reachable for these kinds' after contract, but never specifies the decision rule: what condition on `kind` gates the new envelope path, what function/API from the expand-phase seam is called for op-cert kinds, and what happens when the op-cert producer is unavailable (the dependency gate is on a not-yet-landed branch). The current `sign_manifest` has no kind-dispatch at all. The plan's 'changed' bullet names the effect but not the protocol.
findings.location: What section ('changed: signing.sign_manifest (the expand phase's dual-shape window closes: the HMAC branch for op-cert kinds is deleted, envelope-only)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the read-path dispatch protocol for op-cert kinds post-contract: name whether `verify_signature` gains a kind-based branch to `registry.verify()`, whether `verify_record` is bypassed for these kinds, or whether a new function is introduced — and co-locate this with AC3.
findings.finding: The plan says the op-cert 'read path' will no longer accept HMAC records post-contract, but never specifies the new dispatch mechanism for `verify_signature` / `verify_record` for op-cert kinds. `verify_record` is HMAC-only with no kind parameter; `verify_signature` calls `verify_record` unconditionally. The plan lists 'the op-cert read path (HMAC records for these kinds no longer verify)' as changed, but the replacement protocol — whether `verify_signature` now calls `registry.verify()` for op-cert kinds, whether `verify_record` gains a kind parameter, or whether a new function is introduced — is never defined.
findings.location: What section ('changed: the op-cert read path (HMAC records for these kinds no longer verify)')

findings.finding: The plan premises that 'the producer signing seam ships (the expand phase: producers mint Ed25519 op-certs, the read path accepts both envelopes and legacy HMAC records)' is the precondition for this contract story, but no Ed25519 signing path for op-cert kinds (plan-review, completion-verifier) is found anywhere in the codebase. `signing.sign_manifest` is pure HMAC with no kind-dispatch to an Ed25519/DSSE path; `attest/hmac_legacy.py` documents itself as the expand phase. The plan's premise that the expand phase is already shipped cannot be verified.
findings.location: Why section: 'Once the producer signing seam ships (the expand phase: producers mint Ed25519 op-certs, the read path accepts both envelopes and legacy HMAC records)'

findings.finding: The plan conditionally deletes the `.signing-key` genesis 'if nothing else consumes it', but probing shows `.signing-key` is generated by at minimum two independent consumers: (1) `_commands/init.py` line 532 generates it unconditionally on `rebar init`, and (2) `signing.signing_key()` generates it on any sign call (including non-op-cert kinds). The plan does not enumerate these consumers or resolve whether they would still need `.signing-key` post-removal, making the deletion condition empirically unverified.
findings.location: What section: 'delete the now-dead `.signing-key` genesis if nothing else consumes it'
findings.suggested_fix: After reading the code, the `.signing-key` file is also generated by `rebar init` and is still needed by any non-op-cert HMAC consumer (e.g. the generic `rebar sign` path). The plan should explicitly say: 'Do NOT delete `.signing-key` genesis from `init.py` or `signing_key()` — these serve non-op-cert consumers.' Only the op-cert-specific registration in `hmac_legacy.py`/`register_legacy_schemes()` is removed.

findings.finding: The plan asserts '.signing-key genesis [should be deleted] if nothing else consumes it', but the codebase shows .signing-key is still consumed by at least three non-op-cert paths: (1) rebar init generates it unconditionally, (2) the generic `rebar sign` CLI (sign_cli → sign_manifest with no kind argument) still HMAC-signs using it, and (3) validate.py (fsck) calls verify_record directly with a key resolved from .signing-key. The 'nothing else consumes it' precondition will almost certainly not hold, meaning this deletion is incorrect as specified and will break the generic signing surface and fsck if executed.
findings.location: What section: '…delete the now-dead `.signing-key` genesis if nothing else consumes it'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'delete the now-dead .signing-key genesis if nothing else consumes it' with a scoped statement: '.signing-key generation is retained because it is still consumed by the generic `rebar sign` command and fsck; only the POLICY entries for plan-review / completion-verifier are removed from registry.POLICY.'

findings.finding: The migration doc's rollback posture references `verify.require_environment` and `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` as existing config toggles that govern the low-security default — but neither appears anywhere in the codebase, suggesting they are either new config surface being silently introduced by this story's migration artifact, or forward-references to unbuilt policy. If new, this is config-surface proliferation attached to a doc-only artifact; if assumed-existing, the doc will be factually wrong.
findings.location: What section — migration artifact bullet: 'required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; absent `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` / `verify.require_environment` unset keeps low-security behavior'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the rollback posture sentence with the concrete existing behavior: 'Rollback: since `require_plan_review` defaults to `false` and pre-existing HMAC attestations read as not-certified (not as errors), environments that have not opted into the claim gate see no behavioral change; re-issuing certs is opt-in.' Remove references to `verify.require_environment` and `trusted_environments.yaml` unless those config keys are explicitly added in this story.

findings.finding: The migration doc's rollback posture asserts that `verify.require_environment` (unset) and absence of `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` keep the low-security default, but neither config key nor file path exists anywhere in the codebase — they are not implemented in `_config_schema.py`, `VerifyConfig`, or anywhere else searched.
findings.location: What section — migration artifact rollback posture: 'required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; absent `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` / `verify.require_environment` unset keeps low-security behavior'

findings.finding: The contract phase removes HMAC as the registered scheme for `plan-review` and `completion-verifier` kinds, but the plan contains no acceptance criterion verifying that the prerequisite Ed25519/asymmetric scheme is registered for those kinds before (or atomically with) the removal. If the expand-phase story's scheme registration is absent at deployment time, both op-cert kinds will hit `unknown_scheme` → fail-closed on all new attestation attempts — a silent signing regression with no test catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 / What section ('producers mint Ed25519 op-certs, the read path accepts both envelopes and legacy HMAC records')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: '… and the replacement asymmetric (Ed25519/SSHSIG) scheme is registered for both kinds so `registry.verify(kind, …)` never returns `unknown_scheme` post-contract.' Add a test asserting `registry.resolve('plan-review').scheme != 'HMAC-SHA256'` AND `registry.get_scheme(registry.resolve('plan-review').scheme) is not None`.

findings.finding: The plan's 'delete the HMAC sign/verify branches in signing.py used by op-certs' is ambiguous about shared functions. compute_signature and sign_manifest in signing.py are shared — they serve both the op-cert gates (with kind='plan-review'/'completion-verifier') AND the generic `rebar sign` path (sign_cli calls sign_manifest with no kind). If the implementer deletes these shared functions to remove the 'HMAC sign path reachable for these kinds', they break the generic signing surface (out-of-scope). If they leave them intact, the HMAC signing path remains reachable for op-cert kinds via the generic command (no kind guard). The plan does not specify how to handle this shared-function boundary.
findings.location: What section: 'delete the HMAC sign/verify branches in signing.py used by op-certs (compute_signature callers for these kinds)'

findings.finding: The conditional deletion of `.signing-key` (a step described in 'What') has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion to verify the outcome, leaving the end-state indeterminate: neither deletion nor retention is tested, so the plan's union of steps does not fully prove the stated end-state.
findings.location: What section (`.signing-key` deletion) / Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)
findings.suggested_fix: If deleting: add to AC1 — 'and the `.signing-key` genesis function/call is absent from `signing.py` (verified by `grep -rn \'signing.key\' src/rebar/signing.py` returning no matches).' If retaining: note the rationale in the plan text so no AC is needed.

findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a deliverable — 'MCP surface: sign_manifest / verify_signature operate exclusively on the op-cert seam for these kinds; their docs/help updated' — but no acceptance criterion covers it. There is no AC asserting that the MCP surface docs/help have been updated or that sign_manifest/verify_signature behave exclusively on the Ed25519 path for op-cert kinds.
findings.location: What section ('MCP surface: sign_manifest / verify_signature...'); Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A test (or docstring/help-text assertion) confirms that `sign_manifest` and `verify_signature` for `plan-review` / `completion-verifier` kinds route only through Ed25519 post-contract, and that their CLI help text / docstrings contain no HMAC references for these kinds.'

findings.finding: `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` is named as a new artifact but the parent directory `docs/migrations/` does not exist (only `docs/migrations.md` exists as a flat file). No mention is made of creating the directory, and the AC test asserting the file's existence will fail if the directory is absent.
findings.location: What section: 'Migration artifact `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md`'; AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Either rename the artifact to `docs/hmac-opcert-removal.md` (alongside existing top-level docs) or explicitly state the plan creates the `docs/migrations/` directory.

findings.finding: The decision rule 'validity is computed on read' is stated in the plan's What section but is not co-located with the schema that needs it: compute_validity's own docstring says 'The caller runs the HMAC verify first (verify_signature); this layers the gate semantics on top' — meaning compute_validity only runs on ALREADY-CERTIFIED attestations. After HMAC removal, a stored HMAC op-cert will fail the verify_signature step (verified=False), so claim_gate_check short-circuits before compute_validity runs at all (line 824). The plan's 'validity is computed on read' language implies compute_validity is the mechanism, but the actual mechanism is the signing/registry verdict step. The implementer needs to know whether the 'not-certified' outcome for a legacy HMAC record is produced by (a) verify_signature → not verified, short-circuit in claim_gate_check, or (b) some new compute_validity branch — the plan leaves this ambiguous.
findings.location: What section ('validity is computed on read'); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:556-563 (compute_validity docstring); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:823-829 (claim_gate_check short-circuit)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
